findings.title: Share mint pays $1/share while price can be ~1/Q_SCALE, enabling vault drain
findings.description: place_bet treats market.probs[i] as a fixed marginal price and mints shares = usdc_amount * Q_SCALE / price_scaled (lines 254–274), where price_scaled = p_i * (10000+FEE_BPS)/10000. claim_winnings pays gross = position.shares in USDC base units (lines 423–430, 453), i.e. $1 per share. For the minimum allowed non-zero p_i = 1, integer division yields price_scaled = 1, so shares = usdc_amount * 1_000_000. A 1 USDC stake mints ~1_000_000 USDC of claimable liability. liquidity_b and market.q are stored/updated but never used in pricing or payout, so there is no LMSR cost-function bound on outstanding liability.
findings.exploit_scenario: LP vault holds ≥1M USDC. Attacker creates or uses a market with probs[j]=1, calls place_bet(outcome_idx=j, usdc_amount=1_000_000), receives ~1e12 shares, then after that outcome resolves calls claim_winnings and withdraws ~1M USDC for a 1 USDC cost (minus settlement fee on net winnings).
findings.recommendation: Price shares with a real LMSR cost function using q and b so cost(Δq) is paid in USDC and winning shares redeem at $1 with inventory-consistent liabilities. Reject p_i below a minimum that keeps max leverage within vault capital.
findings.confidence_reason: shares = usdc*Q_SCALE/price_scaled with p_i=1 → price_scaled=1 (FEE_BPS int-div), claim pays shares as USDC (minus settle fee on net).
findings.vote_rationale: Share mint vs $1/share claim is live vault-drain math.

findings.title: update_prediction binds oracle to an arbitrary pool, not to the market
findings.description: Market has no pool field (lines 776–788), and market PDAs are seeded only as [b"market", match_id]. UpdatePrediction requires oracle to sign and checks oracle.key() == pool.oracle_authority, but never constrains that market belongs to pool. Pool PDAs are per-mint ([b"pool", usdc_mint]). An untrusted caller can create any SPL mint, call initialize_pool with themselves as oracle_authority, then invoke update_prediction with that attacker-controlled pool and any victim market.
findings.exploit_scenario: Victim pool exists for real USDC with honest oracle_authority. Attacker creates mint M', calls initialize_pool(M', oracle_authority=attacker). Attacker calls update_prediction with pool=attacker_pool, market=victim_market, and extreme new_probs. Victim market prices move; attacker place_bets on the mispriced side against the shared LP vault.
findings.recommendation: Store pool: Pubkey on Market at create_market. Seed markets as [b"market", pool.key(), match_id]. On UpdatePrediction, add constraint = market.pool == pool.key(). Authorize against that pool's oracle_authority only.
findings.confidence_reason: Auth is oracle == pool.oracle_authority only; Market has no pool field and UpdatePrediction never links market to pool, so any self-owned pool works.
findings.vote_rationale: Missing market.pool lets any-pool oracle reprice any market.

findings.title: Settlement oracle of any pool can resolve any market and drain victim vault
findings.description: Same missing market↔pool binding. Under real-txline, post_txline_attestation and settle_via_txline only check that settlement_oracle matches the supplied pool.settlement_oracle. Attestation PDA seeds are [b"attestation", market.key()] with no pool key. ClaimWinnings also does not bind market to pool; it pays from vault derived from the supplied pool. An attacker who inits a pool with themselves as settlement_oracle can attest and resolve any market to a chosen bin, then claim from the victim pool vault by passing the victim pool into claim_winnings.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker inits pool for mint M' with settlement_oracle = attacker. Attacker place_bets on victim market outcome i via the real USDC pool/vault. Attacker post_txline_attestation + settle_via_txline with attacker pool, choosing stat_value in bin i. Market status becomes Resolved. Attacker claim_winnings with the real USDC pool and vault → drains LP funds.
findings.recommendation: Bind each market to exactly one pool (field + PDA seeds). On PostTxLineAttestation, SettleViaTxLineReal, and ClaimWinnings, require market.pool == pool.key(). Ensure pause is evaluated for the market's home pool.
findings.confidence_reason: Real attest/settle check oracle against the passed pool only; claim_winnings pays from the passed pool vault with the same unbound market, so attacker-pool resolve + victim-pool claim drains.
findings.vote_rationale: Any-pool settlement_oracle resolves market; claim from victim vault.

findings.title: Permissionless create_market allows attacker-chosen probabilities
findings.description: create_market is callable by any signer (CreateMarket.creator) with no pool.authority or oracle check. The creator supplies initial_probs subject only to sum ≈ Q_SCALE (± outcome_count). A two-outcome market with initial_probs = [1, Q_SCALE-1] is valid and sets p_0 = 1, enabling the share-mint vault drain (F-002). Markets are not bound to a pool in state (Market has no pool field); PlaceBet/ClaimWinnings pair any market PDA with the single pool vault for the USDC mint, so one malicious market taxes the whole LP vault. Untrusted inputs: initial_probs, bin_edges, match_id, outcome_count. Path: create_market → place_bet share mint → settle → claim_winnings vault transfer.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker calls create_market(match_id, …, initial_probs=[1, 999_999], liquidity_b=1), bets the minimum USDC on outcome 0, waits for (or influences via settlement path) outcome 0 to win, and claims a payout orders of magnitude above stake from the shared vault.
findings.recommendation: Restrict create_market to pool.authority or oracle_authority; store pool on Market and constrain PlaceBet/ClaimWinnings to that pool; enforce a minimum per-outcome probability and/or maximum shares-per-USDC at creation time.
findings.confidence_reason: CreateMarket is any signer + unpaused pool; initial_probs only need sum≈Q_SCALE so p_i=1 is valid; Market has no pool field and vault is pool-scoped.
findings.vote_rationale: Permissionless create_market accepts attacker probs; enables F-003 drain.

findings.title: Exposure cap tracks stake-in, not payout liability
findings.description: MAX_MARKET_EXPOSURE is documented as a per-market USDC exposure ceiling to stop a market draining the shared LP vault. place_bet only does exposure += usdc_amount and requires new_exposure ≤ MAX_MARKET_EXPOSURE (lines 247–252). Outstanding claimable liability is position.shares (paid in claim_winnings at lines 423–430), which can be up to ~Q_SCALE× larger than usdc_amount when p_i is tiny. exposure is never increased by shares, never reduced on claim, and never compared to vault.amount or pool.total_liquidity.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker places bets on a near-zero-priced outcome. Each bet adds only usdc_amount to exposure (e.g. total exposure = 10 USDC) while minting ~10M USDC of shares. After resolution, claim_winnings pays shares from the shared vault, draining LP funds far beyond the exposure counter.
findings.recommendation: Track per-outcome (or max-outcome) liability as sum(shares) and require liability ≤ min(MAX_MARKET_EXPOSURE, vault free capital). On each bet, delta_liability must pass the cap before minting.
findings.confidence_reason: exposure only adds usdc_amount against MAX_MARKET_EXPOSURE; liability is shares which can be ~Q_SCALE× stake.
findings.vote_rationale: Exposure tracks stake-in only; liability is shares.

findings.title: Unauthenticated publish_stat_root lets anyone anchor a malicious Merkle root
findings.description: publish_stat_root (lines 27–44) accepts any authority: Signer and inits the stat-root PDA for match_id with an arbitrary merkle_root. There is no allowlist, multisig, or program-level TxLINE key check—only first writer wins via init. validate_stat then treats that root as authoritative. Tabula's mock settlement path CPIs into this program and resolves the market from the receipt. Trust boundary is therefore whoever first published stat-root for this match_id, not TxLINE.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker watches for a new market with match_id = M. Before any honest publisher, they call publish_stat_root(M, R) where R is the Merkle root of a tree containing a leaf for a V that maps to the outcome they bet on. They place bets, then call settle_via_txline with a valid proof. Market resolves to their bin and they claim_winnings.
findings.recommendation: Gate publish_stat_root on a fixed TxLINE authority (config PDA / upgrade authority / hardcoded pubkey). Until then, treat mock settlement as fully attacker-controlled whenever the attacker can race publish_stat_root.
findings.confidence_reason: publish_stat_root only requires a signer; no authority/allowlist check before writing merkle_root for match_id.
findings.vote_rationale: Any signer publishes Merkle root; default mock settlement trusts it.

findings.title: real-txline settlement never verifies TxLINE; keeper attestation is sole trust root
findings.description: Under feature = "real-txline", settle_via_txline (lines 374–408) does not CPI to TxODDS/txoracle, does not check a Merkle proof, and does not verify any signature over TxLINE transcript data. Instruction args _txodds_fixture_id, _txodds_seq, and _txodds_stat_key are unused. Checks are only: settlement_oracle signer equals pool.settlement_oracle, market still Trading, attestation age ≤ MAX_RECEIPT_AGE_SEC, attestation match_id/stat_type/stat_value match, and attestation's stored oracle key matches pool. post_txline_attestation lets that same oracle write arbitrary stat_value into a per-market PDA.
findings.exploit_scenario: Compromised settlement_oracle (or malicious keeper) calls post_txline_attestation with V_attacker then settle_via_txline within 15 minutes. Market resolves to the bin containing V_attacker. Keeper's correlated positions claim vault funds. No on-chain check can detect that TxLINE never attested V_attacker.
findings.recommendation: On the production path, CPI into the real TxLINE/txoracle program (or verify an ed25519 signature over match_id, stat_type, stat_value, fixture_id, seq from a known TxLINE key). Do not treat keeper-written PDAs as evidence of Merkle validation.
findings.confidence_reason: real-txline settle only checks settlement_oracle + attestation PDA fields/freshness; no Merkle proof or TxODDS CPI (txodds_* args unused).
findings.vote_rationale: real-txline trusts settlement_oracle attestation only.

findings.title: update_prediction can set near-zero prices and instantly underprice liability
findings.description: oracle_authority may set arbitrary new_probs and new_b with the same weak normalization as create_market (sum ≈ Q_SCALE). new_b is written to liquidity_b but never read in place_bet. After an update that sets some p_i = 1, any bettor can mint maximal shares per USDC under F-003. Existing positions keep prior shares; only the pricing for new bets changes—there is no re-marking or solvency check against vault balance.
findings.exploit_scenario: Compromised or buggy oracle posts new_probs with p_win = 1 for an outcome that is about to settle. Attacker place_bets into that outcome for a few USDC, mints huge shares, and after resolve drains the vault. Even an honest oracle lowering a longshot price creates a mempool race to buy underpriced liability.
findings.recommendation: When updating probs, enforce min p_i, recompute and enforce global liability vs vault equity, and/or pause betting around updates. Prefer LMSR inventory pricing so oracle probs steer b/prior without directly setting executable price to 1/Q_SCALE.
findings.confidence_reason: update_prediction (oracle-gated) can set any normalized probs including p_i=1, enabling the same mint/payout path as F-003.
findings.vote_rationale: update_prediction can set p_i=1 enabling F-003 mint.

findings.title: Market is not bound to Pool; pause and pool context are bypassable at settlement
findings.description: Market stores no pool pubkey. CreateMarket takes a pool account but never records it; market PDA seeds are only [b"market", match_id]. Both settlement account structs load an arbitrary pool PDA and an arbitrary market PDA with no constraint linking them. settle_via_txline only uses pool for require!(!pool.paused) and, on the real path, pool.settlement_oracle. A caller can pass any unpaused pool while resolving the global market for match_id.
findings.exploit_scenario: Admin pauses the main USDC pool after suspecting bad settlement data. Attacker uses another unpaused pool. On mock-txline, with a root they control (F-007), they settle the main market. When admin unpauses, winners of the forced outcome drain the main vault via claim_winnings.
findings.recommendation: Store pool on Market at creation and constrain market.pool == pool.key() in settle, claim, bet, and attestation accounts. Consider including pool.key() in market PDA seeds.
findings.confidence_reason: Market has no pool field; settle only requires !pool.paused on the passed pool, so any unpaused pool bypasses pause on the intended one.
findings.vote_rationale: Pause checked on caller-chosen pool; home-pool pause bypassable.

findings.title: No vault solvency check before minting or paying; shared vault cross-market contagion
findings.description: deposit_liquidity credits pool.total_liquidity and transfers into the pool vault. place_bet also transfers stake into the same vault but never requires vault.amount ≥ outstanding shares on any outcome. claim_winnings transfers payout with only PDA signer seeds, with no accounting debit to total_liquidity/exposure/fees_accrued. All markets share one vault per pool. Insolvency in one market's share inventory competes for the entire LP pot.
findings.exploit_scenario: LPs deposit 2M USDC. Attacker over-mints 1.5M USDC liability on market A via F-001–F-003 and claims after A resolves, emptying most of the vault. Honest winners on market B then fail token::transfer or receive nothing.
findings.recommendation: Maintain reserved_liability per market and globally; place_bet must require vault.amount ≥ reserved_liability + new_shares. On claim, debit reserves and total_liquidity. Consider per-market vaults.
findings.confidence_reason: claim_winnings transfers payout with no vault.amount check; markets unbound to pools so one vault backs many markets.
findings.vote_rationale: No vault solvency vs share liability; cross-market contagion.

findings.title: Mock settle_via_txline is permissionless; any holder of a valid proof can resolve
findings.description: SettleViaTxLineMock requires only payer: Signer. Unlike the real path, there is no settlement_oracle check. Any account that can supply a proof accepted by txline_mock::validate_stat can move a market from Trading to Resolved. Combined with F-007 (open publish_stat_root), permissionless settle means there is no second gate before vault payouts become claimable.
findings.exploit_scenario: After publishing a malicious root (F-007), attacker does not need the pool's settlement_oracle key. They submit settle_via_txline as an ordinary payer as soon as their bets are booked.
findings.recommendation: If mock mode is only for demos, document that it is not production-safe. For any shared validator/devnet value, either require settlement_oracle on the mock path as well, or fix F-007 and add an optional allowlist/timelock before resolve_market.
findings.confidence_reason: Mock settle_via_txline only requires a signer payer; publish_stat_root is also ungated, so a first-writer root + proof fully controls resolution.
findings.vote_rationale: Mock settle permissionless; with F-007 anyone resolves.

findings.title: Permissionless initialize_pool allows front-running canonical mint authority
findings.description: InitializePool lets any signer create the sole pool PDA for a given usdc_mint (seeds = [b"pool", usdc_mint.key()]) and set authority, oracle_authority, and settlement_oracle with no allowlist, multisig, or one-time deployer gate. If the intended operator does not atomically deploy + initialize for the production USDC mint, an attacker can init first and fully control set_paused / rotate_oracle.
findings.exploit_scenario: Program is deployed; operators plan a later initialize_pool for mainnet USDC. Attacker observes deploy and submits initialize_pool with attacker as authority and both oracles. Honest team cannot create the intended pool PDA for that mint.
findings.recommendation: Initialize in the same transaction as deployment, or require a fixed deployer pubkey / multisig as authority signer constraint, or use a config PDA initialized once by program upgrade authority.
findings.confidence_reason: initialize_pool is open to any signer and pool PDA is [b"pool", usdc_mint], so first writer owns that mint's pool.
findings.vote_rationale: First-writer initialize_pool owns mint's authority and oracles.

findings.title: Unauthenticated /predict returns pricing vectors that feed on-chain LMSR
findings.description: POST /predict has no authentication, authorization, mTLS, API key, or request/response signing. Any network client that can reach the process may submit fully attacker-controlled history, live, bin_edges, and base_b and receive probs_q6 and liquidity_b. Architecture states these values are consumed by the keeper and written on-chain via update_prediction. Default listen address is 0.0.0.0; Uvicorn is started without TLS.
findings.exploit_scenario: Oracle is reachable (default bind, misconfigured compose port, shared VPC). Attacker POSTs crafted /predict bodies to map TabFM outputs, or MITMs keeper→oracle HTTP and returns probs_q6 concentrated on an outcome they are buying so the keeper's next update_prediction misprices the market.
findings.recommendation: Require a shared secret (Bearer/API key) or mTLS on /predict; terminate TLS between keeper and oracle; bind to 127.0.0.1 by default; optionally sign responses and verify in the keeper.
findings.confidence_reason: /predict has no auth/middleware and _run_uvicorn defaults HOST to 0.0.0.0.
findings.vote_rationale: Unauthenticated /predict on 0.0.0.0 is a live API surface.

findings.title: Receipt UI presents hardcoded mock Merkle data as authentic on-chain settlement proof
findings.description: ReceiptPanel is marketed as Verifiable Resolution / TxLINE data receipt. loadDemoReceipt never reads chain state, never calls an RPC, and never runs keccak/Merkle verification. It always injects fixed client-side values (stat_value: 5, ellipsized merkle_root / tx_signature, and three static sibling hashes including 0xdeadbeef…). The UI renders those fields as a real receipt and only tells the user to Verify yourself without performing or gating on any check.
findings.exploit_scenario: After a match, a user clicks Fetch receipt expecting cryptographic assurance. The app always shows stat_value = 5 and a fabricated proof/root/tx, regardless of on-chain truth, inducing false confidence that settlement was verified.
findings.recommendation: Load receipt fields from on-chain StatReceipt / StatRoot PDAs, recompute the leaf and walk the Merkle path in the browser, or show a clear unverified demo banner and fail closed with INVALID if proof does not match.
findings.confidence_reason: loadDemoReceipt hardcodes stat_value/merkle_root/proof/tx_signature; UI copy presents them as on-chain TxLINE receipts with no chain read or verify.
findings.vote_rationale: Hardcoded receipt presented as on-chain proof without demo label.

findings.title: Load failure silently falls back to deterministic mock while still reporting ready
findings.description: On ModuleNotFoundError or any other load exception, _load sets self._backend = "mock" and continues. There is no fail-closed mode: the process still serves /predict via _mock_predict. /readyz treats any non-"unloaded" backend as ready, so mock is ready. Mock probabilities are fully deterministic given inputs.
findings.exploit_scenario: TabFM fails to load (OOM, missing extra, HF failure). Oracle keeps serving. Attacker polls unauthenticated /health, sees mock, then reproduces _mock_predict offline and trades against the mis-specified LMSR.
findings.recommendation: Fail closed in production: if TabFM cannot load, exit non-zero or set backend to a non-ready state and return 503 from /predict and /readyz. Have the keeper refuse model_backend == "mock" before submitting update_prediction.
findings.confidence_reason: Load errors set backend = mock; /readyz is ready whenever backend is not unloaded (logs exist, so not fully silent).
findings.vote_rationale: Load failure falls back to mock while /readyz stays ready.

findings.title: Oracle /predict response trusted without schema or economic validation
findings.description: callOracle posts to ORACLE_URL/predict and returns data as PredictResponse with no checks. index.ts uses the response for drift, metrics, and the submit path (would.submit.update_prediction). Nothing verifies probs_q6 length vs bin_edges, sum ≈ Q_SCALE, non-negative probs, liquidity_b bounds, or match_id echo. Note: Anchor signing is still TODO, but the trust boundary is already established for when signing is wired.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker compromises TabFM or intercepts oracle HTTP. They return skewed probs_q6. Once the keeper wires Anchor update_prediction, it signs and posts those probs, moving LMSR prices in the attacker's favor.
findings.recommendation: Validate response shape and invariants before any signing: length, Q6 sum within on-chain tolerance, liquidity_b > 0, match_id binding. Pin oracle identity (mTLS / signed responses).
findings.confidence_reason: callOracle returns data as PredictResponse with no schema/economic checks; index.ts still has TODO and only logs would.submit.update_prediction.
findings.vote_rationale: Unvalidated oracle response; on-chain impact when submit TODO removed.

findings.title: TxLINE SSE live stats drive predictions with no integrity checks
findings.description: In runRealFeed, SSE payloads from TxLineClient.streamScores() are cast to any and filtered only by fixtureId. Live features are taken with ?? defaults and passed into callOracle. txlineClient.ts parses SSE data: lines with JSON.parse and yields them unchecked. There is no signature, sequence monotonicity, timestamp freshness, or range validation on live fields.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker with ability to poison the scores stream (or a malicious TxLINE response) sends fixtureId matching TXODDS_FIXTURE_ID with fabricated cornersSoFar / minute. Keeper calls oracle on false live state and, when wired, signs a misleading market update.
findings.recommendation: Treat SSE as untrusted input: validate types/ranges, enforce seq/timestamp monotonicity per fixture, cross-check snapshots via fetchScoresSnapshot, and require authenticated/paid tokens for production.
findings.confidence_reason: streamScores yields JSON.parse payloads; runRealFeed treats them as any with defaults and feeds them into callOracle with no integrity checks.
findings.vote_rationale: Unvalidated SSE feeds callOracle; dangerous once submit wired.

findings.title: Unauthenticated metrics/health bind all interfaces and leak prediction timing
findings.description: startMetricsServer creates an HTTP server with no authentication and listens on cfg.metricsPort with no host bind (Node defaults to all interfaces). /metrics exposes tabula_last_prediction_ms, tabula_ensemble_divergence, prediction/settlement counters, and match labels. index.ts updates lastPredictionMs and ensembleDivergence on every successful oracle response, before the drift gate and the intended update_prediction submit.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker on the same VPC/host network scrapes http://keeper:9464/metrics. When tabula_last_prediction_ms advances and divergence is high, they race bets before the keeper's update_prediction lands.
findings.recommendation: Bind metrics to 127.0.0.1 (or a private scrape network), require mTLS/bearer auth, and avoid exporting pre-submit timing/divergence on a public interface.
findings.confidence_reason: srv.listen(cfg.metricsPort) binds all interfaces with no auth; /metrics exposes tabula_last_prediction_ms and tabula_ensemble_divergence.
findings.vote_rationale: Unauth metrics leak pre-submit timing; impact rises when submit wired.

findings.title: Unauthenticated /metrics, /model, and /health expose backend and operational state
findings.description: GET /metrics, GET /model, and GET /health are unauthenticated. They reveal whether the live backend is tabfm-pytorch vs mock, device, ensemble size, model id, and Prometheus series. FastAPI's default /docs and /openapi.json are also enabled, documenting the full predict schema to any client.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker discovers the oracle port, reads /health or /metrics, learns the service is on mock (or under load), then prioritizes front-running or timing attacks around predict latency patterns.
findings.recommendation: Keep probes on a separate internal listener or protect /metrics and /model with the same auth as /predict. Disable OpenAPI UI in production.
findings.confidence_reason: /health, /model, and /metrics are open and return backend (and related mode metadata).
findings.vote_rationale: Unauth health/model/metrics expose backend mode.

findings.title: Unbounded score_diff and client-chosen base_b fully steer model outputs
findings.description: LiveState.score_diff and inherited HistoryRow.score_diff have no ge/le bounds (unlike minute, possession, etc.). base_b accepts up to 10_000_000_000 and flows into dynamic_b, directly controlling returned liquidity_b. Combined with F-008, any client can push extreme feature values into TabFM's in-context fit to search for adversarial probability mass. Note: base_b is bounded (ge=1, le=10_000_000_000), so it is client-chosen but not fully unbounded.
findings.exploit_scenario: Unauthenticated client sweeps extreme score_diff / history compositions and base_b values against /predict to find feature settings that maximize probability on a chosen bin, then uses that mapping when poisoning keeper inputs or forging responses.
findings.recommendation: Bound score_diff to realistic ranges. Cap base_b to an operator-configured allowlist (or ignore client base_b). Add sanity checks on returned probs_q6 in the keeper.
findings.confidence_reason: score_diff is unbounded and inputs are client-chosen, but base_b is constrained (ge=1, le=10_000_000_000), so unbounded overstates that field.
findings.vote_rationale: Client-chosen features/base_b steer unauthenticated /predict.

findings.title: HTTP error bodies embedded in exceptions; log redaction misses opaque API tokens
findings.description: Failed TxLINE calls throw Error strings that include await res.text() (guest-start, activate, stat-validation). Callers log failures as String(e). activateApiToken also returns the raw apiToken to the caller. log.ts redacts JWT-shaped strings, sk-…, long base58, and "secretKey": […] but not opaque X-Api-Token values or arbitrary bearer tokens.
findings.exploit_scenario: TxLINE returns an error page or JSON that echoes the Authorization header or apiToken. Keeper logs failures; operator log drain is readable by a lower-privileged role, which reuses the token.
findings.recommendation: Do not append full response bodies to errors; log status codes only. Never return/log apiToken/jwt. Extend redaction for apiToken, X-Api-Token, and Bearer values.
findings.confidence_reason: Guest/activate/stat-validation errors embed await res.text(); log redacts JWT/sk-/long base58/secretKey arrays but not opaque apiToken-style values.
findings.vote_rationale: Error bodies may leak tokens; redaction incomplete.

findings.title: fetchStatValidation returns API proofs/stat values with no local verification
findings.description: fetchStatValidation GETs /scores/stat-validation and returns StatValidationResponse (roots, proofs, statToProve) typed but never verified. keeper/src/merkle.ts implements the mock verifier's leaf/sort-pair scheme but is not used by the client. On the real-txline program path, post_txline_attestation / settle_via_txline trust the keeper's settlement-oracle signature for stat_value without on-chain TxODDS proof checks. Settlement wiring to this API is latent (emulator path does not call it yet).
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker compromises TxLINE stat-validation HTTP (or MITMs it). Response claims statToProve.statValue = N with fabricated proofs. Keeper posts post_txline_attestation / settles with N, resolving the market to the wrong bin.
findings.recommendation: Before any settlement attestation, locally verify Merkle proofs against an on-chain or independently anchored root. Never attest solely from REST JSON. Bind attestation to fixture id, seq, and stat key.
findings.confidence_reason: fetchStatValidation returns API proofs as-is with no local Merkle verify; settlement does not call it yet (emulator only logs), so REST-trust is latent, not live.
findings.vote_rationale: fetchStatValidation unused; settlement not wired to it.

findings.title: Untrusted oracle JSON is rendered as authoritative market probabilities and odds
findings.description: fetchPrediction POSTs to ORACLE_URL and trusts response fields without schema, range, or attestation checks: probs_float, liquidity_b, ensemble_divergence, latency_ms, model_backend. Those values flow into priceWithFee, MarketPanel odds, and OracleStatus. Wallet place_bet is not implemented yet (MarketPanel only alerts), so this does not currently construct drainable transactions.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker controls or intercepts the oracle endpoint. They return probs_float skewed toward a bin they favor. The UI shows attractive decimal odds on that bin. When betting is wired to place_bet, users act on manipulated prices.
findings.recommendation: Treat oracle responses as untrusted: validate types/lengths, prefer HTTPS and authenticated/attested predictions, and use on-chain update_prediction account as source of truth for displayed prices.
findings.confidence_reason: page.tsx maps data.probs_float etc. into odds with no validation; MarketPanel bet clicks only alert(...) and do not call place_bet.
findings.vote_rationale: place_bet is alert-only; no fund path today.

findings.title: shares as i64 truncation when updating market.q
findings.description: place_bet does market.q[i] = market.q[i].checked_add(shares as i64). shares is u64; the as i64 cast truncates values > i64::MAX to a negative i64 without a checked conversion. Today q is never read for pricing or payout (dead inventory), so funds are not directly affected—but any future LMSR using q would inherit corrupted state.
findings.exploit_scenario: If pricing is later wired to q without fixing the cast, an attacker mints shares > i64::MAX (possible with tiny p_i and large stake), corrupts q to a negative or wrapped value, and manipulates subsequent LMSR prices.
findings.recommendation: Use i128 for q inventory, or require shares ≤ i64::MAX via try_from before add. Prefer computing cost in u128 throughout.
findings.confidence_reason: shares as i64 can wrap if shares>i64::MAX, but under MAX_MARKET_EXPOSURE max shares≈1e18 fits i64; q is write-only and unused for pricing.
findings.vote_rationale: market.q unused for pricing/payout today.

findings.title: AdminOnly relies on body checks only; no has_one on pool.authority
findings.description: AdminOnly marks authority as Signer and validates the pool PDA seeds, but does not use has_one = authority. Authorization is only in instruction bodies via require_keys_eq!(authority, pool.authority) for set_paused and rotate_oracle. Both current admin entrypoints perform the check, so there is no live bypass today. The pattern is fragile: any future instruction reusing AdminOnly without copying the body check would be fully callable by any signer.
findings.exploit_scenario: A later admin instruction is added with Context<AdminOnly> but omits require_keys_eq. Any wallet can pause the pool or rotate oracles.
findings.recommendation: Add has_one = authority on pool in AdminOnly (with authority: Signer), and remove redundant body checks or keep them as defense-in-depth.
findings.confidence_reason: AdminOnly has no has_one; set_paused / rotate_oracle do require_keys_eq on pool.authority today—fragile, not currently open.
findings.vote_rationale: Body checks enforce pool.authority today; no live bypass.

findings.title: Merkle leaf omits match_id; safety depends entirely on StatRoot PDA binding
findings.description: Leaf preimage is only stat_type || stat_value.to_le_bytes(), not bound to match_id. Correctness relies on StatRoot PDA seeds [b"stat-root", match_id], receipt copying root.match_id, and Tabula checking account.match_id == market.match_id. Today Tabula checks match_id after CPI, so exploitability requires a regression or alternate consumer of validate_stat.
findings.exploit_scenario: A later integration settles market B while passing stat_root/receipt for match A where (stat_type, stat_value) also appears, and forgets the match_id equality check. The proof verifies and the wrong match's stat resolves market B.
findings.recommendation: Domain-separate leaves as keccak(match_id || stat_type || stat_value_le) so proofs are unusable under the wrong root even if account checks are incomplete.
findings.confidence_reason: Leaf is exactly keccak(stat_type || stat_value_le); match_id is only copied from the stat-root PDA into the receipt.
findings.vote_rationale: match_id checked after CPI today; future footgun only.

fix_clusters.name: LMSR pricing / vault drain
fix_clusters.note: Implement real LMSR or min-price + liability caps; restrict create_market.

fix_clusters.name: Market↔pool binding
fix_clusters.note: Store pool on Market; constrain all privileged and payout instructions.

fix_clusters.name: Settlement trust
fix_clusters.note: Gate mock publish_stat_root; CPI/verify TxLINE on real path.

fix_clusters.name: Oracle/keeper integrity
fix_clusters.note: Auth /predict; validate responses; fail-closed on mock; validate SSE before submit.
